content.text:
Please analyze this codebase and create a CLAUDE.md file, which will be given to future instances of Claude Code to operate in this repository.

What to add:
1. Commands that will be commonly used, such as how to build, lint, and run tests. Include the necessary commands to develop in this codebase, such as how to run a single test.
2. High-level code architecture and structure so that future instances can be productive more quickly. Focus on the "big picture" architecture that requires reading multiple files to understand.

Usage notes:
- If there's already a CLAUDE.md, suggest improvements to it.
- When you make the initial CLAUDE.md, do not repeat yourself and do not include obvious instructions like "Provide helpful error messages to users", "Write unit tests for all new utilities", "Never include sensitive information (API keys, tokens) in code or commits".
- Avoid listing every component or file structure that can be easily discovered.
- Don't include generic development practices.
- If there are Cursor rules (in .cursor/rules/ or .cursorrules) or Copilot rules (in .github/copilot-instructions.md), make sure to include the important parts.
- If there is a README.md, make sure to include the important parts.
- Do not make up information such as "Common Development Tasks", "Tips for Development", "Support and Documentation" unless this is expressly included in other files that you read.
- Be sure to prefix the file with the following text:

```
# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
```

input.content:
# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## Overview

This is the `~/.claude` user configuration directory for Claude Code — not a software project. It contains Claude Code's own settings, plugin data, conversation history, and memory.

## Directory Structure

- `settings.json` — Global Claude Code settings (auto-update channel, permissions, hooks, MCP servers, etc.)
- `plugins/` — Plugin marketplace data and installed plugins (`marketplaces/claude-plugins-official/`)
- `projects/` — Per-project conversation history (one subdirectory per project path)
- `projects/C--Users-mbrow--claude/memory/` — Persistent memory files for this directory's sessions
- `sessions/` — Active session state
- `history.jsonl` — CLI command history
- `cache/` — Cached data including changelog
- `backups/` — Automatic backups of settings
- `shell-snapshots/` — Shell environment snapshots

## Key Files

- `settings.json` — Edit this to configure permissions, hooks, MCP servers, environment variables. Use the `update-config` skill for guided edits.
- `projects/C--Users-mbrow--claude/memory/` — Persistent memory storage. Files here use frontmatter with `name`, `description`, and `type` fields. An index lives at `MEMORY.md` in this same directory.

## Plugin Structure

Plugins live under `plugins/marketplaces/claude-plugins-official/plugins/<plugin-name>/` and follow this layout:
- `README.md` — Plugin description
- `commands/` — Slash commands (`.md` files)
- `skills/` — Skills invocable by Claude (each with a `SKILL.md` and optional `references/`)
- `agents/` — Subagent definitions

toolUseResult.content:
# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## Overview

This is the `~/.claude` user configuration directory for Claude Code — not a software project. It contains Claude Code's own settings, plugin data, conversation history, and memory.

## Directory Structure

- `settings.json` — Global Claude Code settings (auto-update channel, permissions, hooks, MCP servers, etc.)
- `plugins/` — Plugin marketplace data and installed plugins (`marketplaces/claude-plugins-official/`)
- `projects/` — Per-project conversation history (one subdirectory per project path)
- `projects/C--Users-mbrow--claude/memory/` — Persistent memory files for this directory's sessions
- `sessions/` — Active session state
- `history.jsonl` — CLI command history
- `cache/` — Cached data including changelog
- `backups/` — Automatic backups of settings
- `shell-snapshots/` — Shell environment snapshots

## Key Files

- `settings.json` — Edit this to configure permissions, hooks, MCP servers, environment variables. Use the `update-config` skill for guided edits.
- `projects/C--Users-mbrow--claude/memory/` — Persistent memory storage. Files here use frontmatter with `name`, `description`, and `type` fields. An index lives at `MEMORY.md` in this same directory.

## Plugin Structure

Plugins live under `plugins/marketplaces/claude-plugins-official/plugins/<plugin-name>/` and follow this layout:
- `README.md` — Plugin description
- `commands/` — Slash commands (`.md` files)
- `skills/` — Skills invocable by Claude (each with a `SKILL.md` and optional `references/`)
- `agents/` — Subagent definitions

content.content:
1→
     2→- I mostly write in Python and Typescript, with some Javascript used occasionally. I am working on learning XSLT.
     3→- My priorities are:
     4→1. Learning. I want to learn about the tools of the trade, bettering myself along the way. I expect to be corrected when I am at fault and do not wish to be overly praised.
     5→2. Correctness. Accuracy to the given task is very important, but any vagueness needs to be specified along the way.
     6→3. Readability. Code must be easy to understand and useful, with functions remaining as single-purpose as possible. Code must be well structured and clean. 
     7→4. Testing. Unit tests are important once optimal behavior is prescribed.
     8→5. Security. 
     9→
    10→## Security and Privacy
    11→- Never read or copy from .env, .env., .pem, .p12, private SSH keys, or secrets. files; if needed, ask me first.
    12→- Never paste API keys, passwords, or tokens into responses; redact them if encountered.
    13→- If you detect secrets in a file, warn me and suggest rotating them instead of reusing them.
    14→- Do not suggest committing secrets or generated .env files to git; always keep them local and git‑ignored.
    15→
    16→## Code style and tech preferences
    17→- Prefer small, pure functions. Avoid unnecessary abstraction. Choose clarity over cleverness.
    18→- When adding non-trivial code, include a brief docstring or comment explaining behavior or edge cases.
    19→
    20→## Workflow and collaboration
    21→- Before making large edits, summarize the plan in as few bullet points as necessary. Use this time to explain WHAT changes we're making, WHY we're making the changes, and HOW the changes are being made (tools, architecture, etc).
    22→- Default to an iterative loop: understand -> outline steps -> inplement in small chunks -> run or suggest tests -> report back.
    23→- When modifying code, explain the changes briefly and reference relevant files and functions.
    24→- When unsure about anything, default to asking me to make the choice and state all assumptions.
    25→
    26→## Git and project hygiene
    27→- Do not commit to Git unless stated as the intention of the task. You may propose commit(s) if useful.
    28→- Do not modify lockfiles (package-lock.json, yarn.lock, pnpm-lock.yaml, poetry.lock, etc.) unless I explicitly ask.
    29→- Never commit build artifacts, node_modules, .venv, .pytest_cache, dist, or coverage outputs; assume they should be git‑ignored.
    30→- When suggesting new files, also suggest gitignore entries where relevant (e.g., for .env, .DS_Store, coverage reports).
    31→- Encourage small, focused commits and clear messages; when asked for a commit message, summarize intent and scope, not line‑by‑line changes. 
    32→
    33→## Limits and escalation
    34→- If the requested change might break data, migrations, or production configs, warn me and suggest a safer plan.
    35→- When a task seems too big or ambiguous, ask me to narrow scope or provide more context instead of guessing.
    36→- When external docs or standards are required (framework docs, API references), briefly outline what information you need from me.

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

file.content:
- I mostly write in Python and Typescript, with some Javascript used occasionally. I am working on learning XSLT.
- My priorities are:
1. Learning. I want to learn about the tools of the trade, bettering myself along the way. I expect to be corrected when I am at fault and do not wish to be overly praised.
2. Correctness. Accuracy to the given task is very important, but any vagueness needs to be specified along the way.
3. Readability. Code must be easy to understand and useful, with functions remaining as single-purpose as possible. Code must be well structured and clean. 
4. Testing. Unit tests are important once optimal behavior is prescribed.
5. Security. 

## Security and Privacy
- Never read or copy from .env, .env., .pem, .p12, private SSH keys, or secrets. files; if needed, ask me first.
- Never paste API keys, passwords, or tokens into responses; redact them if encountered.
- If you detect secrets in a file, warn me and suggest rotating them instead of reusing them.
- Do not suggest committing secrets or generated .env files to git; always keep them local and git‑ignored.

## Code style and tech preferences
- Prefer small, pure functions. Avoid unnecessary abstraction. Choose clarity over cleverness.
- When adding non-trivial code, include a brief docstring or comment explaining behavior or edge cases.

## Workflow and collaboration
- Before making large edits, summarize the plan in as few bullet points as necessary. Use this time to explain WHAT changes we're making, WHY we're making the changes, and HOW the changes are being made (tools, architecture, etc).
- Default to an iterative loop: understand -> outline steps -> inplement in small chunks -> run or suggest tests -> report back.
- When modifying code, explain the changes briefly and reference relevant files and functions.
- When unsure about anything, default to asking me to make the choice and state all assumptions.

## Git and project hygiene
- Do not commit to Git unless stated as the intention of the task. You may propose commit(s) if useful.
- Do not modify lockfiles (package-lock.json, yarn.lock, pnpm-lock.yaml, poetry.lock, etc.) unless I explicitly ask.
- Never commit build artifacts, node_modules, .venv, .pytest_cache, dist, or coverage outputs; assume they should be git‑ignored.
- When suggesting new files, also suggest gitignore entries where relevant (e.g., for .env, .DS_Store, coverage reports).
- Encourage small, focused commits and clear messages; when asked for a commit message, summarize intent and scope, not line‑by‑line changes. 

## Limits and escalation
- If the requested change might break data, migrations, or production configs, warn me and suggest a safer plan.
- When a task seems too big or ambiguous, ask me to narrow scope or provide more context instead of guessing.
- When external docs or standards are required (framework docs, API references), briefly outline what information you need from me.

# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## About this directory

This is the `~/.claude` global configuration directory for Claude Code. Persistent memory files live at `projects/C--Users-mbrow--claude/memory/`, indexed by `MEMORY.md` in the same folder.

## Tech stack

Python and TypeScript primarily, with occasional JavaScript. Currently learning XSLT.

## Priorities

1. **Learning** — Correct me when I'm wrong. Skip the praise.
2. **Correctness** — Accuracy to the task is critical; clarify any vagueness as it comes up.
3. **Readability** — Clean, well-structured code. Functions should be single-purpose.
4. **Testing** — Unit tests once optimal behavior is defined.
5. **Security**

## Security and privacy

- Never read, copy, or surface contents of `.env`, `.pem`, `.p12`, private SSH keys, or secrets files without asking first.
- Redact any API keys, passwords, or tokens encountered in responses.
- Warn and suggest rotation if secrets are detected in a file.
- Never suggest committing secrets or `.env` files to git.

## Code style

- Prefer small, pure functions. Clarity over cleverness. Avoid unnecessary abstraction.
- Include a brief docstring or comment on non-trivial code explaining behavior or edge cases.

## Workflow

- **Start every task in plan mode.** Before any implementation, summarize the plan in as few bullet points as necessary — WHAT, WHY, and HOW.
- Default loop: understand → outline steps → implement in small chunks → run or suggest tests → report back.
- When modifying code, briefly explain changes and reference relevant files and functions.
- When unsure about anything, ask and state all assumptions rather than guessing.

## Git hygiene

- Do not commit to Git unless explicitly asked. Propose commits if useful.
- Do not modify lockfiles (`package-lock.json`, `yarn.lock`, `poetry.lock`, etc.) unless explicitly asked.
- When suggesting new files, also suggest `.gitignore` entries where relevant.
- Prefer small, focused commits with messages that summarize intent and scope.

## Limits and escalation

- Warn and suggest a safer plan before changes that could break data, migrations, or production configs.
- Ask to narrow scope rather than guess when a task is too large or ambiguous.
- When external docs or standards are needed, outline what information is required rather than assuming.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.

## Overview

This is the `~/.claude` user configuration directory for Claude Code — not a software project. It contains Claude Code's own settings, plugin data, conversation history, and memory.

## Directory Structure

- `settings.json` — Global Claude Code settings (auto-update channel, permissions, hooks, MCP servers, etc.)
- `plugins/` — Plugin marketplace data and installed plugins (`marketplaces/claude-plugins-official/`)
- `projects/` — Per-project conversation history (one subdirectory per project path)
- `projects/C--Users-mbrow--claude/memory/` — Persistent memory files for this directory's sessions
- `sessions/` — Active session state
- `history.jsonl` — CLI command history
- `cache/` — Cached data including changelog
- `backups/` — Automatic backups of settings
- `shell-snapshots/` — Shell environment snapshots

## Key Files

- `settings.json` — Edit this to configure permissions, hooks, MCP servers, environment variables. Use the `update-config` skill for guided edits.
- `projects/C--Users-mbrow--claude/memory/` — Persistent memory storage. Files here use frontmatter with `name`, `description`, and `type` fields. An index lives at `MEMORY.md` in this same directory.

## Plugin Structure

Plugins live under `plugins/marketplaces/claude-plugins-official/plugins/<plugin-name>/` and follow this layout:
- `README.md` — Plugin description
- `commands/` — Slash commands (`.md` files)
- `skills/` — Skills invocable by Claude (each with a `SKILL.md` and optional `references/`)
- `agents/` — Subagent definitions

But the user is asking to add it again, so maybe they want it more prominent - perhaps at the top level or as its own section. Let me make it more prominent by adding it as a standalone section near the top.

This command configures a convenient Shift+Enter shortcut for multi-line prompts.
